title: The Rise of Early Cities
article: Across the ancient world, small farming communities gradually grew into cities. This change took place in different regions at different times. In Mesopotamia, the Nile valley, the Indus basin, and parts of China, people left scattered farms and began to live together. The process began more than five thousand years ago. New farming methods allowed more food to be produced on the same land. As harvests grew, not everyone had to work the fields full time. Some people chose to live in denser settlements near water or trade routes. The meeting of farmers, traders, and craftspeople made such places useful. In time, these settlements gained larger populations and new social roles. Archaeologists call these places the first cities, and they shaped how later societies lived.

Several key changes made early cities possible. First, improvements in irrigation and crop management raised food output. When harvests became more reliable, surplus food could support non-farming workers. Second, trade began to spread goods over longer distances. Traders brought raw materials and finished items that local farmers could not make. Third, craft specialization grew as people focused on tasks like pottery or metalwork. Skilled workers lived in towns where they could sell their goods. Fourth, leaders developed ways to organize labor and collect resources. Temples, chiefs, or councils managed large projects such as canals and granaries. These institutions helped cities survive and expand. Each factor fed into the others and pushed settlements toward urban life.

Early cities shared several common features. Most had a central area used for public life. This area often contained a temple, an administrative building, or both. Large walls or moats protected many cities from attack. Streets sometimes formed simple grids, and markets stood near the center. Evidence of workshops shows that craftsmen worked in neighborhoods. Pots, tools, and metal objects come from these places in large numbers. Another feature was written records, which first appeared to record trade and taxes. Writing helped officials manage resources and keep agreements. Social division became clear as well. Wealthy families, priests, and administrators lived apart from ordinary farmers and laborers. This division influenced the architecture and the distribution of goods.

The move to urban life brought both gains and problems. Cities supported new kinds of art, learning, and technology. Large populations allowed ideas to spread quickly and made complex projects possible. At the same time, cities created new pressures on land and resources. Dense living made the spread of disease easier. Inequality often increased, since some people controlled more land and wealth than others. Cities also relied on food supplies from surrounding regions, tying rural life to urban centers. Over centuries, the model of city life helped form states and empires. It shaped laws, religions, and economic systems that still influence the modern world. Understanding the rise of early cities helps explain how people moved from simple villages to the complex societies we know today.
